notable_deeds.headline: First named lord of Mattingley — recorded in the Pipe Roll of Henry II
notable_deeds.story: In 1167, the royal financial accounts of King Henry II — compiled in the Pipe Roll for the thirteenth year of his reign — record Ellis as owner of the village of Mattingley in Hampshire. He is the earliest named person connected to the manor, standing at the head of a line that would carry its place-name as a surname across 850 years and two oceans. The village itself had been called 'Matingelege' since before the Domesday survey of 1086, deriving from 'Matta's ley' — the woodland clearing of a Saxon chieftain named Matta whose people, the Basingas, had settled the Whitewater Valley in the fifth or sixth century. Ellis left no patronym; the surname was still the land itself.

notable_deeds.headline: Earliest dated bearer of the Mattingley surname — granted 6 virgates and a mill to the Prior of Merton
notable_deeds.story: Stephen de Mattingley is the first person in recorded history to carry the surname in a dated document. In 1206, he appeared in the Curia Regis Rolls as 'Stephen de Madingel'' and in the Feet of Fines for Hampshire (Trinity 8 John) when he granted to Walter, Prior of Merton, and his successors in free alms: six virgates of land, thirty acres, several meadows, six shillings and three pence rent, and a mill at Mattingley. He had also received his brother Bartholomew's share of their father Revelendus's estate. The Oxford Dictionary of English Surnames (Reaney and Wilson, 1991) cites this precise transaction as the earliest dated attestation of the Mattingley surname. The mill and the meadows are gone; the name endures.

notable_deeds.headline: Last lord of Mattingley to bear the name — sold the manor and ended the family's territorial connection
notable_deeds.story: Son of Stephen, Peter de Mattingley was the last of his line to hold the manor that gave his family its name. Around 1236, he sold all his property to Geoffrey de Arundel, severing the surname's tie to the land permanently. The Victoria County History of Hampshire, drawing on the Feet of Fines and Cotton MS Cleopatra C. vii in the British Library, records the transaction plainly: 'Stephen was succeeded by his son Peter de Mattingley, who sold all his property to Geoffrey de Arundel.' After that sale, the name disappears from the village's manorial record for nearly four hundred years. Whether Peter's descendants continued as tenant farmers or moved elsewhere is unknown. A blank of three centuries separates him from the next confirmed Mattingley — a London burial in 1641.

notable_deeds.headline: Secured 'Mattingly's Hope' — 300 acres in Maryland under the patent of September 4, 1666
notable_deeds.story: Thomas Mattingly II arrived in Maryland before 1665 with his wife Elizabeth and their children, part of a wave of English Catholics drawn to Lord Baltimore's promised haven. His father Thomas I had died in England before making the crossing. On September 4, 1666, a formal land patent was issued in St. Mary's County for 300 acres — fifty acres for each of six family members who had emigrated — naming Thomas II and his brother Cezar as patentees. The tract was called 'Mattingly's Hope.' Thomas's name appears on the St. Mary's County Rent Rolls from 1663 to 1720, a continuous fifty-seven-year span of documented residence. His will, probated before January 12, 1715, named children including Ignatius, James, and others who would carry the Catholic Maryland line for two more generations before a branch walked west into Kentucky.

notable_deeds.headline: Co-patentee of 'Mattingly's Hope' — co-founder of the Maryland Catholic line
notable_deeds.story: Cezar Mattingly crossed from England to Maryland with his brother Thomas II, and on September 4, 1666, his name was inscribed alongside Thomas's on the patent for 'Mattingly's Hope' in Charles County, Maryland. While Thomas II eventually settled primarily in St. Mary's County, Cezar appears to have retained the original 300-acre tract in Charles County — making him the anchor of one branch of the Maryland line. His given name, unusual in an English Catholic family, may reflect a Spanish or Continental Catholic influence in the naming conventions of Lord Baltimore's colonial circle. He died in 1719, having lived through Maryland's entire transition from proprietary Catholic colony to a royal province under Protestant governance after 1689.

notable_deeds.headline: Patriarch of the Kentucky migration — his sons carried the Catholic Maryland line westward in 1785
notable_deeds.story: Born around 1704 in St. Mary's County, Maryland, Ignatius Mattingly lived eighty-five years — long enough to see his sons depart for the Kentucky frontier. His will, probated April 1789 in St. Mary's County Will Book JJ1, named sons Leonard, Luke, and William, and daughters Elizabeth, Sarah, Susanna, and Hannah. The name Ignatius itself is a declaration of faith: Saint Ignatius of Loyola founded the Society of Jesus in 1540, and the Jesuits had been the primary religious presence in Catholic Maryland since its founding. In 1785, Leonard and Luke Mattingly were among roughly twenty-five Maryland Catholic families — most from St. Mary's County — who migrated to Nelson County, Kentucky, establishing what became known as the 'Holy Land of Kentucky.' Ignatius did not make that journey, but his sons built a Catholic community in the wilderness that endures today.

notable_deeds.headline: Led the 1785 Catholic Maryland migration to Kentucky; confirmed genetic ancestor of the DNA surname project
notable_deeds.story: Leonard Mattingly Jr. was born in 1764 in Leonardtown, St. Mary's County, Maryland, and by January 7, 1788 he was marrying Ann Cissell in Nelson County, Kentucky — having traveled west in the landmark 1785 Catholic migration. That year, roughly twenty-five Maryland families from St. Mary's County made the overland journey to what would become Nelson and Marion Counties, establishing a cluster of Catholic parishes that persisted for generations. Leonard settled in Marion County in the community of Saint Mary, where he died in 1843. He is the earliest confirmed genetic ancestor in the Mattingly-Matney Y-chromosome DNA Surname Project, with multiple independent participants tracing their direct paternal descent to him. The FamilySearch record for his 1788 Kentucky marriage (V5ZD-6SJ) is his surviving documentary signature.

notable_deeds.headline: Bardstown, Kentucky distillery founded by a descendant of the 1785 Catholic migration
notable_deeds.story: In 1877, J.G. Mattingly built the Mattingly & Moore Distillery — Registered Distillery No. 272, Nelson County, Kentucky — one half-mile south of Bardstown, in the heart of bourbon country. The location was no accident: the 1785 Catholic Maryland migration had transformed this corner of Kentucky into a tight-knit community of farming and commercial families with deep roots in Maryland's tobacco economy. The distillery operated continuously until Prohibition forced its closure in 1920. A present-day descendant operation, J. Mattingly 1845, in Frankfort, Kentucky, claims seven generations of family involvement across eight different distilleries — a commercial lineage stretching from the colonists who planted tobacco in St. Mary's County to the craftsmen who barrel-age whiskey today.

notable_deeds.headline: District Judge of the 22nd Judicial District for 17 uncontested years — from Brunswick army family to Texas bench
notable_deeds.story: Hans Teichmueller was born on March 7, 1837 in Brunswick, Germany, to an army officer and a mother from the noble von Girsewald family — whose members included two generals. His father's death left the family in financial difficulty; in 1856, at nineteen, Hans emigrated to Texas. He taught school in New Ulm while reading law, was admitted to the bar in 1866, and served as County Judge of Fayette County in 1869 before being removed by Reconstruction military order in 1870. In 1884 he was elected District Judge of the 22nd Judicial District. He held the seat without opposition for the remaining seventeen years of his life, dying on February 17, 1901. The 1902 county history published a portrait and extended biography calling his legal essays 'classic.' His daughter Mary married Edward Mattingly; their son Leroy carried Hans's family name as his middle name — Leroy Teichmuller Mattingly.

notable_deeds.headline: WPA muralist; painted 'The Law, Texas Rangers' for the Smithville post office in 1940 — lived to 99
notable_deeds.story: Minette Teichmueller was born in La Grange, Texas on January 27, 1871 — daughter of District Judge Hans Teichmueller — and died in Nacogdoches on August 28, 1970, ninety-nine years old. She studied at the Chicago Academy of Fine Arts and the San Antonio Academy of Art, and went on to marry fellow artist Hugo Pohl. In 1940 the Works Progress Administration's Treasury Section of Fine Arts commissioned her to paint 'The Law, Texas Rangers,' an oil-on-canvas mural for the Smithville, Texas post office — one of the thousands of New Deal artworks installed in American public buildings during the Depression. The family remembers her as 'really avant-garde for her time,' someone who as a teenager slipped away from home to hear Mark Twain read aloud — a scandalous act in a devout Southern household. She outlived her famous father by sixty-nine years.

notable_deeds.headline: Painted a 36x75-foot stage curtain for San Antonio Municipal Auditorium — destroyed in the 1979 fire
notable_deeds.story: Hugo Pohl painted on a scale that few artists ever attempt. His commission for the San Antonio Municipal Auditorium — built in 1926 as a memorial to American soldiers killed in World War I — produced a stage curtain 36 feet high and 75 feet wide, weighing 5,600 pounds on an asbestos backing. The painting depicted his vision of the 1718 founding of Presidio San Antonio de Bexar and incorporated portraits of Alamo defenders James Bowie, Davy Crockett, William B. Travis, and James Butler Bonham. For more than fifty years it hung in the city's largest public hall. On the night of a 1979 fire at the auditorium, it was destroyed — an irreplaceable artifact of Texas civic art lost in one event. Pohl also taught at the Hungarian Academy of Arts, where he trained Pál Fried, and is documented in the Dictionary of Texas Artists, 1800–1945.

notable_deeds.headline: Bought Reeves County mineral rights in April 1901 — three months after Spindletop — whose royalties the family still receives
notable_deeds.story: In April 1901, Pearl Johnson Baity purchased land in Reeves County, Texas — raw West Texas range, then home to fewer than 2,000 souls. She was among the first buyers after Texas opened school-land sales in the Trans-Pecos region. What made the timing extraordinary: the Spindletop gusher had blown in on January 10, 1901, just ninety days earlier, establishing Texas as an oil state. Pearl retained the mineral rights when she later sold the surface. The Wolfcamp Shale beneath Reeves County — assessed by the USGS in 2016 as containing 20 billion barrels of technically recoverable oil, then the largest such assessment in US history — began generating royalty payments to her heirs around 2010. Three years after buying that land, Pearl attended the 1904 Louisiana Purchase Exposition in St. Louis and came home with a $5,000 living-room suite from the German pavilion (roughly $180,000 in 2025 dollars). She died around 1971, approximately 97 years old.

notable_deeds.headline: Builder credited with constructing a substantial portion of early twentieth-century San Antonio
notable_deeds.story: William Alexander Baity came from Yadkin County, North Carolina to San Antonio in the late nineteenth century and built a contracting business that left a physical imprint on the city. Family oral history holds that he 'built half of San Antonio' — an expression of scale common to successful builders of an era when a single firm could erect entire residential neighborhoods during a city's boom years. He died in 1945 in the house at 211 Castillo Avenue, San Antonio — the home he built around 1905 in the Lavaca neighborhood just south of downtown, in the same corridor as the King William Historic District where prosperous tradespeople of his generation settled. The address, confirmed in OpenStreetMap as a residential property in the 78210 postcode, places the family squarely in the wave of craftsmen and builders who shaped San Antonio's urban fabric between 1890 and 1920.

notable_deeds.headline: Prominent Austin pediatrician; death at the Texan Hotel, January 31, 1934 — covered by AP wire across Texas
notable_deeds.story: Dr. Claude Mattingly was thirty-five years old when he was found dead in his room at the Texan Hotel, Austin, on February 1, 1934. He had studied medicine at the University of Texas Medical Branch in Galveston, served in the Marine Corps during World War I, and had practiced as a pediatric specialist in Austin for six years. The bodies of both Dr. Mattingly and Mrs. Rhea B. Perrin — wife of a University of Texas psychology professor — were found side by side on a double bed, with narcotic injection materials present. Justice of the Peace Tom R. Johnson returned a verdict of double suicide. The story ran on the front page of the Brownsville Herald on February 1, 1934, and was carried by the Associated Press and International News Service across Texas through February 6. Claude's body was returned to his boyhood home of La Grange for burial. His father Ed Mattingly was still living there. The family has not spoken of him since.

notable_deeds.headline: Lived to approximately 107 — one of perhaps five documented American centenarians of his era
notable_deeds.story: Family oral history preserves the memory of a Mattingly great-great-great-grandfather born around 1828 who lived to approximately 1935, reaching age 107. At that time, documented centenarians in the United States could be counted on one hand; a man who remembered the years before the Civil War and lived to witness the New Deal would have been a significant figure in any Texas community. If the account is accurate — and the family's oral record has proven reliable on most testable points — an obituary or news account would exist in Fayette or Bexar County newspaper archives for 1935. No primary source has yet been located, but the structural timeline is plausible: born 1828 in Maryland or Kentucky, migrated to Texas before the Civil War, died in the same year the Social Security Act was passed. Longevity runs conspicuously in this line.

notable_deeds.person_name: Joseph Hardin Frost (president of Frost Bank, 1926)
notable_deeds.headline: President of Frost Bank from 1926 — San Antonio institution founded 1868
notable_deeds.story: Joseph Hardin Frost became president of Frost Bank — formally the Frost National Bank — in 1926. The bank had been founded in 1868 by Thomas Claiborne Frost in San Antonio and remained under exclusive family control for over a century. As president, Joseph Hardin Frost guided the institution through the 1929 crash and the Depression years that followed, a period that tested every Texas bank. Frost Bank survived when many others did not. Family oral history identifies a 'Frost Bank uncle' — likely Joseph Hardin Frost — as a relative through the San Antonio branches of the family. Whether by blood or marriage, the connection places the Mattingly family in the inner circle of San Antonio's financial establishment during the city's formative decades.

examples: Pearl (Paralee) Johnson Baity (~1874–1971): died approximately age 97
The Centenarian ancestor (~1828–1935): reportedly age 107 at death (unverified primary source)
longevity_runs_in_the_family.note: Judith (Mattingly) Turner (1645–1744) and Elizabeth Dorcas Mattingly (1656–1749) also appear in family tradition with reported lifespans of ~99 and ~93 respectively; primary source verification for these two is pending.

key_themes: Catholic identity maintained across eight generations — from Lord Baltimore's Maryland to the Holy Land of Kentucky
Longevity: three near-centenarians or confirmed centenarians across five generations
Art and law: a judge, a muralist, and a monumental painter all within one family generation
Mineral foresight: Reeves County land purchased three months after Spindletop, mineral rights retained through the twentieth century
Suppressed tragedy: a prominent physician's death that the family never discussed again
Anglo-Saxon roots documented in primary sources back to 1167 — village, surname, and DNA all pointing to Hampshire
evidence_compilation: All stories draw from prior research sessions conducted April 2026. Primary sources include: Pipe Roll 13 Henry II (Ellis/Stephen via BHO VCH Hampshire vol. 4); Curia Regis Rolls 1206 and Feet of Fines Hampshire (Stephen de Mattingley); WikiTree Mattingly-60/210 and FamilySearch (Thomas II, Ignatius, Leonard Jr.); Lotto 1902 Fayette County Her History and Her People at archive.org (Hans Teichmueller); Wikipedia articles on Minette Teichmueller and San Antonio Municipal Auditorium (Minette, Hugo Pohl); Brownsville Herald Feb 1-6 1934 and San Antonio Light Feb 2 1934 at LOC Chronicling America (Claude Mattingly); USGS Fact Sheet 2016-3092 and Wikipedia on Spindletop (Pearl Baity land purchase); pre-pro.com DST391 (Mattingly & Moore Distillery); Wikipedia on Frost Bank (Joseph Hardin Frost). The centenarian ancestor entry and the 'built half of San Antonio' characterization rest on family oral history (Shari Mattingly, 2024 interview) and carry lower confidence than the documentary entries.
